sentences.romanized: Karada no guai ga warui desu.

sentences.base_language: I need to see a doctor.
sentences.target_language: お医者さんに診てもらう必要があります。
sentences.romanized: Oisha-san ni mite morau hitsuyō ga arimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜てもらう (te morau) = 'to have someone do something for you / to receive the action'. 診てもらう = 'have a doctor examine me'. This giving/receiving grammar is central to Japanese: もらう (receive), あげる (give), くれる (give to me).

痛い (itai) = 'hurts / is painful'. The body part is the subject with が: 頭が痛い (my head hurts), お腹が痛い (my stomach hurts). No possessive 'my' is needed — just as in Chinese, context makes possession clear.

sentences.base_language: My stomach has been hurting since yesterday.
sentences.target_language: 昨日からお腹が痛いです。
sentences.romanized: Kinō kara onaka ga itai desu.
sentences.grammarNote: から (kara) = 'from / since' a starting point in time: 昨日から (since yesterday), 朝から (since morning). For ongoing states that started in the past, Japanese uses a simple present-tense adjective + から, not a perfect tense as in English.

sentences.target_language: 変なものを食べましたか？
sentences.romanized: Hen na mono wo tabemashita ka?

sentences.grammarNote: 処方する (shohō suru) = 'to prescribe'. In Japanese medical settings, doctors often speak in the volitional 〜ましょう form to express what they will do: 処方しましょう = 'I'll prescribe [for you]'. It sounds decisive but caring.

sentences.base_language: Take this medicine three times a day.
sentences.target_language: この薬を一日三回飲んでください。
sentences.romanized: Kono kusuri wo ichinichi sankai nonde kudasai.
sentences.literal: This medicine one-day three-times take please.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜回 (kai) is the counter for occurrences/times: 一回 (once), 二回 (twice), 三回 (three times). 一日 (ichinichi) = 'per day / one day'. Pattern: 一日三回 (three times a day). 飲む (nomu) is used for both drinking and taking medicine.

sentences.base_language: Rest and drink plenty of water.
sentences.target_language: よく休んで、水をたくさん飲んでください。
sentences.romanized: Yoku yasunde, mizu wo takusan nonde kudasai.
sentences.literal: Well rest, water plenty drink please.
sentences.grammarNote: よく (yoku) before a verb = 'well / properly / plenty': よく休む (rest well), よく食べる (eat well). This is different from よく meaning 'often': よく行く (go often). Context determines the meaning.
